title: Neri: preparing the Japanese formation aid
subtitle: How to extract and use the tororo aoi mucilage that makes nagashizuki sheet forming possible
excerpt: Neri is the viscous formation aid that slows vat drainage in nagashizuki, the Japanese toss-and-pour sheet-forming method. Without it, long-fibre washi pulp drains too fast to form an even sheet. This tutorial covers preparing neri from dried tororo aoi root and using PEO as a synthetic alternative.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), Internet Archive: public domain. Hunter documents the use of formation aid in Japanese washi production, including the tororo aoi root (Abelmoschus manihot). The nagashizuki sheet-forming method and the role of neri within it are described in structural terms; this tutorial synthesises from Hunter's observations alongside the general chemistry of plant mucilages.

glossaryTerms.definition: The viscous formation aid used in nagashizuki Japanese sheet forming. Traditionally prepared from the mucilaginous root of tororo aoi (Abelmoschus manihot); synthetically available as polyethylene oxide (PEO).

The Japanese toss-and-pour sheet-forming method. The papermaker pours pulp and water onto the mould and rocks it back and forth, tossing the surplus off the far edge. The neri slows drainage enough to allow this multi-pass technique.
Paper that has not been sized and therefore absorbs liquid readily. Most freshly formed handmade sheets are waterleaf until surface-sized with gelatin or starch.
The even distribution of fibres across a formed sheet. Good formation means fibres are spread uniformly and the sheet has consistent thickness and strength throughout.

content.text: Western sheet forming relies on fast drainage: the papermaker dips the mould into a vat of dilute pulp and lifts it straight up, letting the water drain through the mesh in seconds. Japanese
nagashizuki

depends on the opposite condition. The papermaker pours pulp onto the mould and rocks it back and forth, building the sheet in layers and tossing the surplus off the far edge. For this to work, the water must drain slowly.
Neri

is the substance that makes this possible. It is a plant mucilage that, when added to the vat in small quantities, thickens the water just enough to slow drainage and allow the long-fibre pulp to spread evenly across the mould. Without it, kozo or other long-fibre pulps drain in seconds and form uneven sheets.

attrs.title: Blender safety
attrs.body: Pulping paper fibre is electrical work near water. Keep the blender unplugged when loading or rinsing it. Drain the vat before reaching in. Never use a kitchen blender that also handles food: paper fibres are not food-safe.

items.name: Dried tororo aoi root (Abelmoschus manihot, Japanese hibiscus)
items.qty: 50 g for a single vat session
items.substitutions: Formation aid (PEO, polyethylene oxide) is a reliable synthetic alternative — see below.

items.name: Cold water
items.qty: 1 litre for soaking the root, plus vat water

items.name: Clean muslin or cheesecloth
items.substitutions: Any fine-mesh cotton cloth. A clean nut milk bag works.

items.name: Paper mould and deckle, A4
items.substitutions: Any mould size works — the neri technique is the same regardless of sheet size.

content.text: The move: preparing neri from tororo aoi

Place the dried tororo aoi roots in a large bowl or jug. Pour in enough cold water to fully submerge them. Soak for at least four hours; overnight gives a more concentrated mucilage. Do not heat the water: heat degrades the mucilage and significantly reduces its viscosity.

After soaking, the water around the roots will have thickened visibly. Take the roots out and squeeze them firmly over the bowl, extracting as much of the mucilage-laden liquid as possible. The roots themselves feel slippery and slightly gelatinous.
Strain the liquid through the muslin cloth into a clean container to remove any fibre fragments or root particles. The strained neri should be slightly viscous and clear to pale amber in colour.

Add the neri to the vat a small amount at a time. Stir well after each addition and test the drainage rate by lifting the mould and watching how fast the water runs through the mesh. The correct concentration makes the water feel slightly slippery on the fingers and causes the vat to drain in 30-60 seconds rather than in a few seconds. It should not feel gel-like or prevent drainage altogether.
Once the vat drains at the right rate, begin the nagashizuki pull. Check the
formation

of the finished sheet to judge the neri concentration. Even thickness across the whole sheet means good formation. A thick edge and thin middle means the concentration was too low. The pulp drained before it had time to spread.
PEO as a synthetic alternative

Polyethylene oxide (PEO), sold as formation aid for hand papermaking, produces the same effect as tororo aoi neri without the preparation step. It comes as a white powder; a small amount dissolved in cold water (typically 1-2 g per 10 litres of vat water) is enough. PEO is odourless, non-toxic at these concentrations, and gives consistent results from batch to batch, which tororo aoi does not always do. The drawback is that PEO degrades in the vat over several hours and the vat water cannot be stored from one session to the next. Make up fresh PEO solution for each papermaking session.
Signs of over-neri

Too much neri in the vat is a common mistake for first-time washi papermakers. The pulp suspension becomes very viscous, the mould drains very slowly or not at all, and formed sheets are thick and wet-feeling even after couching. If this happens, remove some of the vat water and replace it with plain water, then test again. It is much harder to correct an over-neri vat than an under-neri one. Start with less neri than you think you need.

items.problem: Sheets are uneven: thick at one edge, thin at the other
items.cause: Neri concentration too low; the pulp drained before spreading across the mould
items.fix: Add a little more neri to the vat, stir, and test the drainage rate again before pulling another sheet.

items.cause: Over-neri: too much formation aid added
items.fix: Scoop out a third of the vat water and replace with plain cold water. Stir and test.

items.problem: Tororo aoi soak produced very little viscosity
items.cause: Root was old and degraded, or water was too warm during soaking
items.fix: Source fresh dried root from a specialist papermaking supplier. Always use cold water; warm water destroys the mucilage. If root quality is unreliable, switch to PEO.

items.problem: Neri in the vat lost effectiveness mid-session
items.cause: Tororo aoi neri degrades over time, especially in a warm room
items.fix: Top up with fresh neri solution as the session progresses. PEO is more stable but also degrades; use the vat within a few hours of mixing.

content.text: Where it leads
Neri preparation is the step that makes
nagashizuki
sheet forming possible. It produces the even, strong, long-fibre sheets that define traditional
waterleaf

washi. Once you can control the neri concentration reliably, more techniques become possible. Embedded inclusions (flower petals, threads), watermarks, and two-colour layered sheets all rely on the slow, steady drainage that neri provides.
